source.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

claim_chain: Manganese oxides are abundant redox-active minerals with poorly understood reactivity due to structural and oxidation state variations
We quantified reduction kinetics of three geochemically relevant manganese oxides using extracellular electron shuttles
Gibbs free energy described kinetics of individual oxides well, but Pourbaix free-energy difference offered distinct advantages
Coupled kinetic-mass transport modeling showed electron-transfer rate constants varied among oxides while mass-transfer coefficients were similar
Classical nucleation theory indicated that balance between surface and bulk energies controls dissolution barrier
Findings demonstrate how reaction thermodynamics and phase differences jointly control manganese oxide reduction kinetics
Results support a generalizable predictive framework for reactivity of redox-active minerals across diverse environmental conditions

typical_pattern: State the experimental objective or question
Describe the mineral phases or experimental conditions
Present quantitative results with reference to figures
Interpret results in terms of mechanism or thermodynamic framework
Compare across conditions or mineral types
Connect findings to broader implications or theoretical framework

likely_priorities: Validation of thermodynamic calculations and standard reduction potentials
Justification of model assumptions for multielectron systems
Generalizability of findings beyond the three mineral phases studied
Mechanistic clarity on electron transfer pathways and dissolution mechanisms
Statistical rigor and reproducibility of kinetic measurements
likely_attacks: Limited number of mineral phases may not support broad generalizability claims
Uncertainty in reaction pathways for multielectron systems introduces bias in thermodynamic estimates
Peak in rates around pH 8.5 attributed to electrostatic attraction needs more direct evidence
Classical nucleation theory application may oversimplify complex dissolution processes
Duplicate measurements may be insufficient for robust statistical conclusions
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The paper explicitly acknowledges that determination of ΔrGx0′ relies on predefined redox reactions which may introduce biased thermodynamic estimates in multielectron systems where the full set of possible redox pathways is not known. The authors address this by employing Pourbaix potential as an alternative. The paper also notes that the structural transition from manganese oxide to Mn2+ remains less resolved due to the multitude of possible pathways for mineral dissolution. The peak at pH 8.5 is described as likely caused by electrostatic attraction, indicating some uncertainty. Error bars represent SEM from duplicate measurements, suggesting limited replication. The abstract claims a generalizable predictive framework based on three mineral phases.

examples.quoted_phrase: These results suggest that this least exergonic step acts as a common thermodynamic bottleneck
Our findings not only demonstrate how reaction thermodynamics and phase differences jointly control manganese oxide reduction kinetics
This peak was likely caused by electrostatic attraction between the EES and the manganese oxide surface
the Pourbaix free-energy difference (ΔΨ) offered a distinct advantage by predicting reactivity without requiring detailed knowledge on reaction pathways
support a generalizable predictive framework for the reactivity of redox-active minerals across diverse environmental conditions

phrase_evidence.phrase: Manganese (oxyhydr)oxides are abundant redox-active minerals that influence diverse biogeochemical processes
yet their redox reactivity remains poorly understood due to variations in mineral structure and manganese oxidation state
To evaluate the effect of mineral properties on the kinetics of manganese oxide reduction
we selected three manganese oxides that differ in manganese oxidation state and crystal structure
These phases represent the most ubiquitous manganese oxides in soils and sediments
Surface area–normalized manganese reduction rates (rSA) were determined by monitoring changes in the concentration of the reduced EES species
shows that rSA for both hausmannite and manganite increased as pH decreased
whereas acid birnessite exhibited a consistently high rSA
Differences in the pH dependence of rSA between manganese oxides can be attributed to variations in electron–proton coupling
This peak was likely caused by electrostatic attraction between the EES and the manganese oxide surface
as confirmed by surface speciation modeling
while pH influenced rSA, EES identity also modulates reduction kinetics
To capture the combined influence of pH and EES identity on manganese oxide reduction kinetics
we calculated the Gibbs free energy
In alignment with the behavior of iron oxides
the kinetic data correlate most effectively with ΔrGx0′
These results suggest that this least exergonic step acts as a common thermodynamic bottleneck
rSA generally increased as ΔrGx0′ became more negative
the structural transition from manganese oxide to Mn2+ remains less resolved
This is due to the multitude of possible pathways for mineral dissolution
which are inherently more complex than the one-electron reduction characteristic of iron oxides
To address this limitation, we employed the Pourbaix potential
which quantifies the absolute free-energy metric of each oxide under given pH and redox conditions
allowing for direct comparison across oxides without requiring reaction-specific assumptions
Our findings not only demonstrate how reaction thermodynamics and phase differences jointly control manganese oxide reduction kinetics
but also support a generalizable predictive framework for the reactivity of redox-active minerals
Crystal structures of hausmannite, manganite, and acid birnessite
Hydrogen atoms are omitted for clarity
Error bars represent the SEM from duplicate measurements
Rates were determined for the first 10% of the total reaction

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. Numeric values, chemical formulas, and technical terms are directly quoted. No external references, author names, or invented data appear in the output. The profile accurately reflects the paper's mechanistic focus, thermodynamic framework, and balanced claim strength with appropriate hedging.
